original_abstract:
Microbes play a fundamental role in the global carbon cycle through the processing of soil organic matter (SOM), yet the specific molecular dynamics of this system remain largely opaque. Current theories suggest that a significant portion of SOM—particularly the dissolved organic matter (DOM) fraction—is comprised of low-molecular-weight compounds derived from microbial lysates and cellular metabolites. However, characterizing this small-molecule pool and determining how these compounds are partitioned between biological uptake and mineral adsorption remains a major analytical challenge.

In this study, we developed a streamlined untargeted metabolomics workflow and a novel 13C-stable isotope spike-recovery method to characterize the water-extractable organic matter (WEOM) fraction, which represents the most bioavailable component of SOM. We evaluated several mass spectrometry-compatible extraction buffers (including water, K2SO4, NH4HCO3, and various organic solvent mixtures) on both unfumigated and chloroform-fumigated soil samples. Using gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) and a library of authentic standards, we identified 55 primary metabolites. As expected, chloroform fumigation significantly expanded the diversity and concentration of detected metabolites, confirming its efficacy in releasing intracellular microbial contents.

To investigate the fate of these microbial products upon entering the soil matrix, we spiked soil samples with a 13C-labeled lysate from the soil bacterium *Pseudomonas stutzeri* RCH2. This approach allowed us to differentiate exogenous microbial inputs from endogenous soil chemicals with high precision. Our results indicate that the soil matrix acts as a significant sink; only 27% of the added microbial metabolites showed more than 50% recovery in the WEOM fraction. Notably, cationic compounds such as polyamines and most organic anions were rapidly sequestered, suggesting they are likely immobilized by mineral and clay surfaces. This study provides a robust framework for estimating the bioavailability of microbial metabolites and offers new tools for linking microbial metabolic pathways to broader terrestrial carbon cycling.
humanized_abstract:
Microbes are central to the global carbon cycle through the turnover of soil organic matter (SOM). But little is known about the underlying molecular mechanisms. Current dogma suggests that a large portion of SOM—the dissolved organic matter (DOM) fraction—consists of labile, low-molecular-weight carbon byproducts of microbial metabolism that reflect the direct release of microbial lysate and cellular metabolites. However, elucidating the composition of the labile SOM pool and the exact fate of its components—the degree to which they are assimilated by microorganisms versus adsorbed to mineral surfaces—remains a poorly resolved and technically challenging area of research.

We developed an enhanced untargeted metabolomics approach and 13C stable isotope spike-recovery strategy to characterize water-extractable organic matter (WEOM) - the most bioavailable fraction of soil organic matter (SOM). We investigated the effects of a variety of mass spectrometry compatible extraction buffers including water, K2SO4, NH4HCO3 and a number of organic solvent mixtures alone and in combination on unfumigated and chloroform fumigated soils. GC/MS with authentic standards identified 55 primary metabolites and their changes in the soils before and after chloroform fumigation. Chloroform fumigation as expected led to an increase in the number and amount of metabolites detected.

In this study we investigated the fate of these microbial derived products released into the soil ecosystem. We spiked pure culture soil bacterium *Pseudomonas stutzeri* RCH2 soil samples with 13C labeled lysate. Using 13C labeling of the microbial derived compounds, we were able to accurately separate and quantify the contribution of external microbial derived substances from that of soil endogenous organic matter. Our results show that the soil system is an important fate compartment for microbial derived compounds with only 27% of the added microbial derived substances having more than 50% recovery in the WEOM fractions. Many of the cationic compounds including all polyamines and the majority of organic anions appeared to be strongly immobilized by mineral and clay fractions. Our study provides a solid framework for estimating the bioavailability of microbial derived substances released into terrestrial systems and links microbial metabolic processes to terrestrial carbon cycle models.
